title: Performance and Optimization
summary: Profile Java applications and apply targeted optimizations for speed and memory

sections.title: Premature Optimization Is the Root of All Evil
sections.content: Donald Knuth's famous quote warns that optimizing code before understanding what's slow is like repainting a car before fixing the engine. The correct approach is: first make it work, then make it right, then make it fast. Performance work starts with measurement, not guessing. Use a profiler (like VisualVM, JProfiler, or YourKit) to identify actual bottlenecks. You'll often find that 90% of the time is spent in 10% of the code — that's where you should focus. Common bottlenecks include excessive object creation in loops, inefficient data structures, unnecessary I/O, and suboptimal algorithms.

sections.title: String Concatenation: Performance Trap
sections.content: This contrasts slow string concatenation with efficient StringBuilder usage in a loop

sections.title: Profiling, Benchmarking, and JMH
sections.content: Guessing about performance is unreliable because the JVM optimizes code as it runs. The JIT (Just-In-Time) compiler identifies hot methods and compiles them to native code. A method called 10,000 times may run 50x faster than when it first starts. This makes manual timing with System.currentTimeMillis() misleading — it includes JIT warmup. The Java Microbenchmark Harness (JMH) is the proper tool for benchmarks as it handles warmup, dead-code elimination, and statistical analysis. Think of JMH as a scientific instrument for measurement — it controls all variables so you can trust the numbers.

sections.title: Optimizing without profiling first
sections.content: Most common mistake: spending hours micro-optimizing code that runs once and doesn't matter, while ignoring a real bottleneck like an N+1 query or O(n^2) algorithm. Fix: always profile first to identify where the time is actually spent. Wrong: assuming the bottleneck is in method X and optimizing it. Correct: run a profiler, find the hottest method (most CPU time), and optimize that.

sections.title: Your turn
sections.prompt: You have a log processing method that reads a large file line by line, splits each line by comma, and stores fields in an ArrayList. It takes 30 seconds to process a 500 MB file. Without rewriting the entire logic, identify three targeted optimizations you'd investigate using a profiler: one related to data structures, one related to string handling, and one related to I/O buffering.
hints: What data structure offers faster lookups if you're frequently searching by one field?
What happens if the lines have thousands of fields — is split() still efficient?
Does Files.readAllLines() or BufferedReader.readLine() handle large files better?
